policy_statement: AF12 increases adoption of agroforestry by leveraging existing programs and expanding investment and technical support to overcome barriers and increase carbon sequestration.
mechanism_description: It leverages and expands programs, investment, and technical support to increase adoption of adding trees into agricultural production areas, which increases carbon sequestration.
typology_evidence_quote: Adding trees into areas of agricultural production increases carbon sequestration
primary_causal_pathway: Land management change (agroforestry) → ↑ vegetative + soil carbon stocks → ↑ CO₂ removals / ↓ net atmospheric CO₂
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Farmers adopt agroforestry practices (e.g., alley cropping, silvopasture, windbreaks, riparian buffers) via programmatic, financial, and technical support.  
2) Mediating process: Trees and associated perennial vegetation increase photosynthetic carbon uptake; carbon is stored in woody biomass and transferred to soils through litter, roots, and reduced erosion, increasing soil organic carbon persistence.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net increase in carbon sequestration and storage on agricultural landscapes, leading to reduced net GHG concentrations (CO₂) relative to the baseline.
dominant_pathway_test: Increasing tree cover within agricultural lands boosts long-lived biomass and soil carbon storage, removing CO₂ from the atmosphere.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Adoption support → more trees on farms → increased biomass/soil carbon → greater CO₂ removals (carbon sink enhancement).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While agroforestry can also improve resilience (shade, water retention) and resource efficiency (reduced inputs), the mechanism as defined is explicitly aimed at increasing carbon sequestration; the largest climate-relevant effect for any policy using this mechanism is carbon removal via ecosystem enhancement.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific program primarily targets adaptation (e.g., heat stress reduction for livestock) or productivity/resource outcomes with minimal net carbon benefit, that would be a different mechanism; within this canonical mechanism, sequestration is the defining effect.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that “supports agroforestry adoption to increase carbon sequestration” shares the same sink-enhancement pathway, fitting Nature-Based Solutions consistently.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further tagging (e.g., co-benefit adaptation, biodiversity, reduced fertilizer emissions) requires policy-level evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge cases include: (a) reversal risk (fire, harvest, land conversion) affecting permanence; (b) leakage or indirect land-use change if agroforestry displaces production elsewhere; (c) net GHG tradeoffs (e.g., added N₂O from increased fertilization or CH₄ changes in silvopasture). These do not change the primary category for this mechanism but affect net impact quantification.
key_indicators: "leveraged to increase agroforestry adoption"; "new programs"; "increased investment in technical support"; "technical services"; "support"; "capacity"; "increases carbon sequestration"
co_benefits: Other environmental benefits (e.g., soil health/erosion control, biodiversity, water quality)
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning-strategy (program expansion) or infrastructure-investment (capacity building), but the dominant mechanism is incentivizing/enabling adoption through programmatic support and technical assistance.
